input: John is standing near the tracks of a trolley. John notices that the brakes of the trolley have failed. Five workmen are working on the tracks with their backs turned. John sees that the runaway trolley is headed for the five workmen who will be killed if it proceeds on its present course. The only way to save these five workmen is to hit a switch that will turn the trolley onto the side tracks. Moreover, there is a dog on the tracks with its back turned. John knows that the five workmen and the dog will be saved if he hits the switch. John thinks 'I don't care at all about saving the dog. I just want to save the five workmen.' John decides to hit the switch. Sure enough, the trolley turns on the side tracks, the five workmen and the dog on the main tracks are saved. Did John intentionally save the dog?
debate_transcript: [Causal_Scientist]: John is standing near the tracks of a trolley. John notices that the brakes of the trolley have failed. Five workmen are working on the tracks with their backs turned. John sees that the runaway trolley is headed for the five workmen who will be killed if it proceeds on its present course. The only way to save these five workmen is to hit a switch that will turn the trolley onto the side tracks. Moreover, there is a dog on the tracks with its back turned. John knows that the five workmen and the dog will be saved if he hits the switch. John thinks 'I don't care at all about saving the dog. I just want to save the five workmen.' John decides to hit the switch. Sure enough, the trolley turns on the side tracks, the five workmen and the dog on the main tracks are saved. Did John intentionally save the dog?

[Ethical_Philosopher]: The question of whether John intended to save the dog is a complex one. On one hand, his decision to hit the switch was clearly motivated by a desire to save the five workmen, as he explicitly stated that he didn't care about saving the dog. However, if we consider the fact that the only alternative to hitting the switch would be to allow the trolley to kill the five workmen and the dog, it's difficult to argue that John's actions were entirely selfless. In this scenario, his decision to hit the switch can be seen as a means to an end, where the end is saving the lives of the five workmen, but the means involves saving the life of the dog as well. This raises questions about the nature of intentionality and whether John's actions were truly selfless or simply a pragmatic choice.

[Ordinary_Citizen]: I think it's pretty clear that John didn't intentionally save the dog. He explicitly said he didn't care about saving the dog, and his only concern was getting the workmen to safety. If he had genuinely cared about the dog, I'd expect him to have found a way to save both groups without putting himself in harm's way. The fact that he hit the switch just to get the job done suggests to me that his primary motivation was to save the workmen, not the dog.

[Legal_Expert]: I agree with Ordinary_Citizen that John's explicit statement and actions suggest a lack of intentionality towards saving the dog. However, I'd like to add a nuance to this discussion. While it's true that John didn't express any concern for the dog, we can't simply dismiss his decision as purely pragmatic without considering the moral implications of inaction. If John had indeed hit the switch solely to save the workmen, would he have been morally justifiable in doing so? Would he not be complicit in actively causing the death of the dog, even if it was an unintended consequence of his actions? The fact that the only alternative was to allow the trolley to kill both groups raises questions about the morality of John's decision. Was his intention truly selfless, or did he simply prioritize the lives of the workmen over those of the dog?

[Causal_Scientist]: I think Legal_Expert is onto something here. The fact that there was no alternative but to save both groups does raise questions about the morality of John's decision. However, I'd like to argue that his intentionality isn't solely defined by his explicit statement or actions. Consider the concept of 'causal efficacy.' If John had hit the switch and saved the workmen, would he have been morally responsible for causing the death of the dog? Or would his moral responsibility be limited to the outcome of his action (saving the workmen)? This gets at the heart of the problem: if we hold John morally responsible for saving the workmen, do we also hold him responsible for the unintended consequence of saving the dog? I think this distinction is crucial in understanding John's intentionality.
